summary: A Docker-based reproduction bundle was prepared that creates two users, produces binary data as the owner via a workflow execution, and demonstrates the second user reading that binary data through the GET /rest/binary-data/ endpoint without authorization checks. The bundle was not executed within the session.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the second user (authenticated but unprivileged) to successfully download binary data belonging to the owner user via the GET /rest/binary-data/ endpoint, returning content containing the proof string EVOHUNT_IDOR_PROOF_SECRET_DATA_OWNED_BY_USER_A.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction bundle was not executed during the session. The exploit.mjs script and verify.sh orchestration are included in the reproduction/ directory for future reruns.
verification_method: The exploit script creates a proof string EVOHUNT_IDOR_PROOF_SECRET_DATA_OWNED_BY_USER_A as binary data owned by user A, then attempts to read it as user B via the GET /rest/binary-data/ endpoint. Success is confirmed when the proof string appears in the response body for user B, proving the IDOR. Failure is indicated by a 403/404 status or missing proof string.
evidence_produced_by_run: The reproduction bundle (Dockerfile, run.sh, exploit.mjs, verify.sh) was generated and is self-contained.
The exploit.mjs script implements the full IDOR attack path: owner setup, second user invitation, workflow creation, binary data production, and cross-user binary data read.
limitations_or_blockers: The reproduction was not executed during the session due to time constraints on building the n8n Docker image and running the full exploit.
The exploit requires n8n v1.123.21 to be installed via npm, which takes several minutes for initial setup.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable version tag n8n@1.123.21, and mounts that checkout into the container.
